A Systematic Study of Pseudo-Relevance Feedback with LLMs
Nour Jedidi
University of Waterloo
Waterloo, ON, Canada
njedidi@uwaterloo.caJimmy Lin
University of Waterloo
Waterloo, ON, Canada
jimmylin@uwaterloo.ca
Abstract
Pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) methods built on large language
models (LLMs) can be organized along two key design dimensions:
thefeedback source, which is where the feedback text is derived
from and thefeedback model, which is how the given feedback text
is used to refine the query representation. However, the indepen-
dent role that each dimension plays is unclear, as both are often
entangled in empirical evaluations. In this paper, we address this
gap by systematically studying how the choice of feedback source
and feedback model impact PRF effectiveness through controlled
experimentation. Across 13 low-resource BEIR tasks with five LLM
PRF methods, our results show: (1) the choice of feedback model
can play a critical role in PRF effectiveness; (2) feedback derived
solely from LLM-generated text provides the most cost-effective
solution; and (3) feedback derived from the corpus is most benefi-
cial when utilizing candidate documents from a strong first-stage
retriever. Together, our findings provide a better understanding of
which elements in the PRF design space are most important.1
1 Introduction
Modern pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) techniques have increas-
ingly begun to leverage large language models (LLMs) as a tool
for improving query representations. The use of LLMs for pseudo-
relevance feedback has provided notable improvements in effective-
ness compared to traditional approaches which refine query repre-
sentations using relevance signals from top-ranked documents.
Current methods that leverage LLMs for pseudo-relevance feed-
back can be organized along two key design dimensions: thefeed-
back sourceand thefeedback model. The feedback source refers to
where the feedback – i.e., the terms or vectors used to improve the
query – comes from, while the feedback model is how this feedback
is used to update the query representation. LLM PRF methods typi-
cally derive feedback from one of three sources: corpus documents
(corpus only), LLM-generated text (LLM only), or a combination
(corpus & LLM). To update the query representation, the approach
differs based on the downstream retriever. With BM25, various ap-
proaches have been proposed, such as updating via a simple string
concatenation [ 23], adaptively reweighting the query weight based
on the length of the feedback [ 26], or using classical methods [ 9].
With dense retrievers, LLM PRF methods have primarily utilized
a simple average vector update [ 5,8], but other approaches like
Rocchio [19] can be utilized [14].
However, the independent role that the feedback source and feed-
back model play in the downstream effectiveness of PRF with LLMs
remains unclear. Existing LLM PRF approaches often entangle both
dimensions in empirical evaluations: proposed methods commit
to a specific feedback source and feedback model, but compare to

baselines which differ along both dimensions. Not only that, but
other factors, such as the number of feedback terms, number of
feedback documents, or even downstream retriever are not con-
trolled for. Motivated by this, our goal in this paper is tofairly
andsystematicallyevaluate these design dimensions. To do so, we
organize our paper around the following research questions:
RQ1 For a given feedback source, how does the choice of feed-
back model impact PRF effectiveness?
RQ2 Should feedback be derived from high-quality corpus docu-
ments or “fake” LLM-generated documents?
RQ3 Does combining feedback sources improve PRF effective-
ness over using a single source? If so, how should the feed-
back be combined?
RQ4How does the latency vary among LLM PRF methods?
To answer these research questions, we reimplement five LLM
PRF methods which represent each of the three primary feedback
sources (corpus only, LLM only, and corpus & LLM), ensuring, to
the best of our ability, that any confounding factors – like the
number of feedback terms or number of feedback documents – are
controlled for across all methods. We then evaluate each method on
13 low-resource BEIR datasets [ 21], with three different retrievers
– BM25, Contriever, and Contriever MS-MARCO – and various
feedback models. To support reproducibility, our work directly
builds on existing PRF infrastructure present in Anserini [ 25] and
Pyserini [ 14,15], enabling our implementations to be seamlessly
integrated with widely used open-source IR research toolkits. Our
findings can be summarized as follows:arXiv:2603.11008v1  [cs.IR]  11 Mar 2026

•ForRQ1, we find that the feedback model is an important
design choice for methods which derive feedback from LLM-
generated text.
•ForRQ2, our results demonstrate that feedback should gener-
ally be derived from “fake” LLM-generated documents unless
the corpus feedback comes from a strong initial retrieverand
BM25 is utilized as the downstream retriever.
•ForRQ3, with the Contriever models, the results show a clear
advantage when combining feedback sources that are derived
independently and concatenated. For BM25, combining feed-
backcanbe helpful, but its benefit depends on deriving feedback
terms from high-quality corpus documents; beyond that,how
the feedback is combined does not matter.
•ForRQ4, we found LLM only feedback to be the most efficient
across the board. When integrating corpus feedback, the latency
is dependent on the number of candidate corpus documents or
the average document length in the corpus.
Furthermore, our evaluations reveal additional insights, including:
•Utilizing BM25 with LLM only feedback is a surprisingly tough
baseline to beat. Corpus feedback provides meaningful gains if
the LLM integrates more candidate documents, but this comes
at the cost of latency. One can also improve effectiveness by
integrating candidate documents from a strong initial retriever,
but this reduces the relative benefit of PRF.
•Feedback terms derived from high-quality corpus documents
retrieved by HyDE are interestingly more effective than the
feedback terms generated directly by HyDE, boosting effective-
ness by 3.6%.
•Dense retrievers do not fully take advantage of PRF. For exam-
ple, when provided access to thesame exactfeedback content,
PRF with BM25 consistently outperforms PRF with Contriever
MS-MARCO by as much as 8.5%, despite BM25 starting off 10%
less effective than Contriever MS-MARCO.
The core contribution of this work is a large-scale systematic study
that evaluates different LLM PRF methods “under the same roof”,
in which theindividualimpact of the feedback source and feedback
model can be properly understood. By doing so, our results enable
a better understanding of which elements in the PRF design spaceare most critical. We hope our study can provide clarity and guide
best practices for future PRF methods built with LLMs.
2 Background and Related Work
The traditional setup for pseudo-relevance feedback is as follows:
given a query, 𝑞, an initial retriever, 𝑅1, produces a ranked list of doc-
uments,𝐷. From𝐷, the top-𝑘candidate documents, {𝑑1,𝑑2,...,𝑑 𝑘},
are taken to be relevant, forming the set of pseudo-relevantfeed-
back documents, 𝑓PRF. Next,𝑓PRFgets passed to afeedback model
which generates the updated query, 𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤. The specifics of how the
feedback model generates𝑞 𝑛𝑒𝑤is discussed in Section 3.2.
With the advent of LLMs, research has recently shifted to di-
rectly utilizing knowledge contained within these models to assist
in updating the query representation. With LLMs, the core PRF
concept remains: there exists a set of feedback text from which
useful terms (or vectors, in the case of dense retrieval) are extracted
and utilized for enriching the original query representation. The
only difference lies in where the feedback text is derived from.
Early work focused on training LLMs to generate the feedback
text (this is commonly referred to as LLM query expansion in the
literature). One of the initial methods in this space was GAR [ 17],
which trained an LLM to expand queries by generating various
useful expansion texts given the query as input. As LLMs started
demonstrating strongfew-shotandzero-shotcapabilities [ 3], meth-
ods began generating expansions via direct prompting. Approaches
like HyDE [ 5] and Query2Doc [ 23] focused on hypothetical an-
swer document generation, prompting the LLM to generate text
that “looks like” a real document. Researchers also looked into var-
ious other prompting strategies [ 7] and other forms of generated
expansions (e.g., keywords, summaries, or entities) [4, 16].
Follow up methods then began incorporating corpus evidence in
combination with the LLM-generated expansions, either by using
pseudo-relevant documents as in-context examples for hypothetical
answer document generation [ 7,20] or fusing representations inde-
pendently derived from the LLM and the corpus [ 10,12]. Building
on this, ThinkQE [ 13] proposed an approach in which the LLM
iteratively refines the query representation through multiple inter-
actions with the corpus. In parallel, other works like ReDE-RF [ 8]

and LLMF [ 18] instead leveraged LLMs as a tool forselecting, rather
than generating, top-ranked documents from 𝑅1for enriching the
query representation. Recently Bigdeli et al . [2]released QueryGym,
which standardizes the implementation of these various methods
into a singular Python toolkit.
While the core difference between traditional PRF and LLM PRF
lies in where feedback text comes from, how the feedback is uti-
lized for updating the query representation has differed, particularly
with sparse retrievers like BM25 [ 9]. Initially, LLM PRF methods
for BM25 simply generated 𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤via a simple string concatenation,
for example by repeating the query a fixed number of times and ap-
pending it to the generated expansion [ 7,12,20,23]. MuGI [ 26] then
observed the importance of a proper amount of query repetitions
with respect to the expansion content and proposed an adaptive
query reweighting strategy that repeats the query a variable num-
ber of times based on the length of the generated expansions. Most
recently, Jedidi and Lin [9]demonstrated that LLM query expan-
sion methods should instead return to classic feedback models like
RM3 [ 1] and Rocchio [ 19] for updating the query representation,
demonstrating strong improvements compared to recent string
concatenation approaches, including MuGI. In this paper, for up-
dating the query for BM25, we focus primarily on classic feedback
models – and not string concatenation methods – given they are
well-established and have demonstrated strong effectiveness with
LLM PRF methods.
On the other hand, with dense retrievers, there has been more
consistency in how queries are updated. Methods like HyDE and
ReDE-RF only considered a simple average vector update. In this
paper, we extend our evaluation to the Rocchio dense vector update,
as was done in Li et al. [14].
3 Pseudo-Relevance Feedback with LLMs
In this section, we overview each of the different approaches for
generating – or selecting – feedback documents (i.e., the feedback
sources) with LLMs. We then discuss the feedback models that are
considered for updating the query representation in our evaluations.
An overview of the different PRF pipelines is shown in Figure 2.
3.1 Feedback Source
3.1.1 Corpus Only.To leverage an LLM for selecting feedback
documents directly from the corpus, we build upon the approach
utilized by ReDE-RF [ 8]. In particular, given 𝐷, the ranked list of
documents retrieved by the initial retriever, we utilizeUmbrela[ 22]
to generate a relevance label for each document in the top- 𝑘candi-
date documents, i.e., {𝑑1,𝑑2,...,𝑑 𝑘}.Umbrelathen returns a rele-
vance assessment for each document, producing a score between
0-3, where 0 denotes “irrelevant”, 1 denotes “related”, 2 denotes
“highly relevant”, and 3 denotes“perfectly relevant”. The output of
this step is a list,{𝑟(𝑑 1),𝑟(𝑑 2),...,𝑟(𝑑 𝑘)}, where𝑟(𝑑𝑖)denotes the
score produced byUmbrelafor document𝑑 𝑖.
Next, using some heuristic, documents deemed relevant are se-
lected for the feedback set, 𝑓UMBRELA . In our case, for simplicity, and
to avoid the need for hyperparameter tuning, we opt for a simple
heuristic for selecting which documents to include in 𝑓UMBRELA : for
{𝑟(𝑑 1),𝑟(𝑑 2),...,𝑟(𝑑 𝑘)}, for a given 𝑞, we select all documents withthe maximum score amongst all the assessed documents, i.e.,
𝑓UMBRELA ={𝑑 𝑖∈𝑓|𝑟(𝑑 𝑖)=max
𝑑𝑗∈{𝑑 1,𝑑2,...,𝑑 𝑘}𝑟(𝑑𝑗)}.(1)
We refer to this method as PRF-Umbrela.
3.1.2 LLM Only.Forgeneratingfeedback based on the LLM’s para-
metric knowledge, we leverage HyDE [ 5]. HyDE samples 𝑁hy-
pothetical answer documents by zero-shot prompting an LLM to
write a passage that answers the question. The hypothetical doc-
uments, ˆ𝑑𝑖, generated by the LLM form the feedback set: 𝑓HyDE=
{ˆ𝑑1,..., ˆ𝑑𝑁}.
3.1.3 Corpus & LLM.Finally, we consider two methods which com-
bine feedback derived from the corpus and the LLM’s parametric
knowledge. As one of our goals is to understand the improvement
when combining feedback sources versus using a singular feedback
source, we directly build on PRF-Umbrelaand PRF-HyDE so we
can evaluate therelativeboost of augmenting a given feedback
source with the other feedback source (i.e., augment corpus only
feedback with feedback generated by an LLM).
The first method is motivated by the approach used in CSQE [ 12].
We independently run both PRF-Umbrelaand HyDE and then
simply concatenate their feedback sets together, i.e., 𝑓Umbrela-HyDE =
𝑓UMBRELA∪𝑓HyDE . We refer to this method asUmbrela-HyDE.
The second method, which we refer to as PRF-HyDE, simply
extends on the HyDE setup by additionally prompting it using the
top-𝑘candidate documents, {𝑑1,𝑑2,...,𝑑 𝑘}, asin-contextexamples,
i.e., “write a passage to answer the question, using the provided
reference passages as context”. In this case the feedback documents,
𝑓PRF-HyDE , are of the same form as those of HyDE, but instead now
HyDE has access to the language, structure, and facts present in
the corpus.
BothUmbrela-HyDE and PRF-HyDE represent different ways to
combine corpus information with the LLM’s parametric knowledge.
On one hand,Umbrela-HyDE does soexplicitly: corpus vectors
are directly integrated into the feedback model which updates the
query vector. On the other hand, PRF-HyDE does soimplicitly:
the LLM is still responsible for the content it generates but can be
guided by the in-context corpus examples.
3.2 Feedback Models
Given the different sets of feedback – i.e., 𝑓UMBRELA ,𝑓HyDE ,𝑓PRF-HyDE ,
and𝑓Umbrela-HyDE – how does the query get updated? Before de-
scribing the feedback models, for consistency in terminology across
the different feedback sets, let𝑓be the set of feedback documents
that will be used for generating the updated query, 𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤. Let𝑞vec
be the query vector and 𝑑vec[𝑖]be the document vector of the 𝑖th
document in 𝑓. There are differences in the query update for sparse
retrievers and dense retrievers, which are described below.
3.2.1 Sparse retrieval.For updating 𝑞for BM25, we consider two
commonly utilized feedback models in the literature. The first is
the Rocchio algorithm [ 19]. The Rocchio algorithm computes the
weight of a term𝑡in𝑞 𝑛𝑒𝑤,𝑤𝑡,𝑞new, using the following equation:

frequency of 𝑡in𝑑vec[𝑖].𝛼and𝛽are hyperparameters which control

The next feedback model we consider is RM3 [ 1]. We repeat the

where𝜆controls how much weight is given to terms in the query
versus terms in the feedback documents.
We note that each of the feedback documents typically under-
goes some sort of preprocessing which first removes the common
corpus terms and then further prunes the text to a set of candidate
expansion terms based on a term’s aggregated importance (e.g.,
term frequency) across all documents in 𝑓. For this, we directly
follow the implementation procedure in Anserini [ 25], which is
described in more detail by Jedidi and Lin [9]. Once each of the term
weights𝑤𝑡,𝑞newis computed, custom Lucene queries are constructed
in Pyserini [ 15], assigning each term a boost equal to its respective
weight. The Lucene queries constitute 𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤, which gets passed to
the downstream retriever,𝑅 2, for a second round of search.
3.2.2 Dense retrieval.For dense retrieval, we consider both average
vector feedback and Rocchio vector feedback [ 14]. The average
vector simply concatenates the vector, 𝑞vec, with each of 𝑑vec∈𝑓
and takes a simple average:
𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤=Avg(𝑞 vec,𝑑vec[1],···,𝑑 vec[𝑁])(4)
The Rocchio vector update mimics Equation 2:

As before,𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑤then gets passed to 𝑅2for a second round of search.
4 Experimental Setup
To implement PRF-Umbrela, we directly leverage theUmbrela
prompt described in Upadhyay et al . [22] .2Unless otherwise speci-
fied,Umbrelais applied to only the top-10 candidate documents
retrieved by the initial retriever, following previous work [12, 20];
however, we useat mosteight documents to update the query
representation.3This choice matches the number of generated doc-
uments from HyDE and PRF-HyDE, which we describe next. The
use ofat mosteight documents to update the query representation
remains fixed across all experiments.4
For HyDE and PRF-HyDE, we follow the implementation details
outlined by Gao et al . [5]. We generate eight hypothetical docu-
ments using a temperature of 0.7 with a maximum length of 512
tokens. As with PRF-Umbrela, unless otherwise specified, we feed
PRF-HyDE the top-10 retrieved documents as in-context exemplars.
2We modify the prompt for ArguAna to describe the counter-argument retrieval task.
3If all candidates are deemed relevant, we simply select the top-8 returned by𝑅 1.
4We say “at most” since there will be fewer than eight feedback documents in cases
Umbrelafinds<8documents relevant.To handle prompt length limits, we truncate each of the in-context
documents to at most 256 tokens.
As the number of feedback documents forUmbrela-HyDE will
always exceed eight, to match the goal of RQ3 while maintaining a
fair comparison with the above methods, we prioritize documents
identified by PRF-Umbrelaand use HyDE documents to “pad” the
difference between eight feedback documents and the number of
feedback documents identified by PRF-Umbrela. Meaning, for ex-
ample, if PRF-Umbrelayields five feedback documents, we append
three HyDE-generated documents.
We consider three retrieval models across our experiments: BM25,
Contriever [ 6], and its supervised fine-tuned variant, Contriever
MS-MARCO (Contriever MSM). For each retriever, in addition to
the LLM PRF methods, we evaluate traditional PRF baselines which
directly utilize the top-8 retrieved documents (to match the number
of maximum feedback documents used by all LLM PRF methods)
as the set of feedback documents. We also evaluate each retriever
without PRF.
All retrieval experiments were performed in Pyserini [ 15] with
default parameters. Qwen3-14B [ 24] served as the backbone LLM
and all LLM inference was performed using vLLM [11].
We evaluate all approaches on 13 low-resource retrieval datasets
from BEIR [ 21]. The tasks include news retrieval (TREC-News,
Robust04), financial question answering (FiQA), entity retrieval
(DBpedia), biomedical IR (TREC-Covid, NFCorpus, BioASQ), fact
checking (SciFact, Climate-FEVER), citation prediction (SciDocs),
tweet retrieval (Signal-1M), question answering (NQ) and argument
retrieval (ArguAna). For metrics, we report nDCG@20.
5 Varying the Feedback Model
RQ1 For a given feedback source, how does the choice of feed-
back model impact PRF effectiveness?
To answer the first research question, we evaluate each of the LLM
PRF methods (PRF-Umbrela, HyDE, PRF-HyDE,Umbrela-HyDE)
across various feedback models. As discussed in Section 3, for sparse
retrieval we consider both the Rocchio and RM3 feedback models.
We set the Rocchio parameters to 𝛼=1 and𝛽=0.75 and for RM3 we
set the query weight 𝜆=0.5. Lastly, we limit all feedback models to
at most 128 feedback terms following Jedidi and Lin [9]. For dense
retrieval, we consider both the average vector feedback and Rocchio
vector feedback. For the Rocchio query update, we set 𝛼=0.4 and
𝛽=0.6 following the parameters used in Li et al . [14] . The results for
this experiment can be found in Table 1 for BM25 and Tables 2, 3,
for Contriever and Contriever MSM, respectively. We summarize
the key insights below:
•For BM25 (Table 1), results show a clear benefit of using the
Rocchio feedback algorithm ( ≈1 point improvement, on av-
erage) for HyDE documents versus RM3. This is in contrast
to feedback derived from corpus documents, where the two
methods are similar with PRF-Umbrela(43.3 vs 43.0, for RM3
and Rocchio, respectively), and RM3 is stronger with no LLM
involvement (38.7 vs 37.3).
•For dense retrievers (Tables 2 and 3) using the Rocchio feedback
algorithm improves HyDE’s effectiveness when compared to
the simple average vector approach utilized by Gao et al. [ 5].
In particular, HyDE and PRF-HyDE improve by 4.4 (13.3%)

6 Corpus-Derived vs. LLM-Generated Feedback
RQ2 Should feedback be derived from high-quality corpus docu-
ments or “fake” LLM-generated documents?
In this section, we answer RQ2 by comparing PRF-Umbrela(Feed-
back: Corpus Only) and HyDE (Feedback: LLM Only) across both
sparse and dense retrievers. As the HyDE-generated feedback is
“retriever-free”, it only has access to the downstream retriever ( 𝑅2).
To fairly evaluate PRF-Umbrelaagainst HyDE, we begin by re-
stricting PRF-Umbrelato a single retriever for both initial and
downstream retrieval (i.e., 𝑅1==𝑅 2). We then relax this restriction
by allowing PRF-Umbrelato use any initial retriever while keeping
the downstream retriever fixed to the same𝑅 2as HyDE.
6.1 Results: Single Retriever Available
We begin our analysis by comparing both methods when applied
for expanding BM25 queries, which is shown in Table 1. The results
demonstrate that feedback terms derived directly from LLM hypo-
thetical documents are more effective, making a 1.5-point (3.4%)
improvement over directly using high-quality corpus documents.
The dense retrieval setting (Tables 2 and 3) shows a similar story:
feedback derived from embeddings of LLM-generated hypothetical
documents (HyDE) is more effective than feedback derived solely
from corpus documents. In particular, with Contriever, HyDE makes
a 1.5-point (4.2%) improvement. With Contriever-MSM the improve-
ment is smaller, with only a 0.9-point (2.1%) gain, on average.
However, it remains unclear if the differences in effectiveness
can be attributed to (1) corpus documents having less useful feed-
back terms or (2) other factors such as the fact that PRF-UmbrelaTable 4: Bounding the number of HyDE generations to the

our findings show the following. If restricted to a single retriever,

balances effectiveness and efficiency.
7 Combining Feedback from Corpus & LLM
RQ3 Does combining feedback sources improve PRF effective-
ness over using a single source? If so, how should the feed-
back be combined?
In Section 6 we examined how feedback derived solely from the
corpus compares to feedback generated solely from the LLM’s para-
metric knowledge. We now consider the combination of the two
feedback sources, with a focus on first understanding the effec-
tiveness “boost” of incorporating the additional feedback source
to PRF-Umbrelaand HyDE and then also the optimal strategy for
combining feedback. To do so, in this section, the primary compar-
isons are between (1) PRF-UmbrelaandUmbrela-HyDE; (2) HyDE
and PRF-HyDE; and lastly, (3)Umbrela-HyDE and PRF-HyDE, two
approaches for combining the feedback sources. At a high-level,Um-
brela-HyDE augments the feedback from PRF-Umbrelabyexplic-
itlyconcatenating vectors independently derived by PRF-Umbrela
and HyDE. PRF-HyDE incorporates corpus evidenceimplicitlyby
prompting HyDE with corpus documents as in-context examples.
7.1 Results: Single Retriever Available
As in Section 6.1, we begin in the setting in which a single retriever
is available. Starting with Table 1, we first note that surprisingly,
there is no improvement when prompting HyDE with in-context
examples from BM25, as both HyDE and PRF-HyDE are equally
effective on average (44.5 versus 44.6 nDCG@20). On the other
hand, combining PRF signals from the corpus and LLM via the
Umbrela-HyDE method makes a 2.2-point (5%) improvement on
PRF-Umbrela. However, the improvement ofUmbrela-HyDE upon
PRF-HyDE is minimal, with only a 0.6-point (1.3%) improvement.
With dense retrievers (Tables 2 and 3), we again see no improve-
ment when prompting HyDE with in-context examples. However,
we do find thatUmbrela-HyDE makes a strong improvement in ef-
fectiveness versus PRF-Umbrela, HyDE and PRF-HyDE. For exam-
ple,Umbrela-HyDE makes a 2.9-point (7.8%) and 1.1-point (2.5%)improvement upon PRF-HyDE with Contriever and Contriever
MSM, respectively.
How does integrating more candidate corpus documents impact
accuracy? In Figure 3, the results demonstrate that both PRF-HyDE
andUmbrela-HyDE improve with more candidate documents, with
PRF-HyDE seeing a larger initial benefit. However, the differences
in effectiveness between PRF-HyDE andUmbrela-HyDE versus
PRF-Umbreladecrease as more candidate documents are scored,
with all methods being about equally effective (46.5, 45.9, and 45.9
nDCG@20, respectively).
Interestingly, it is of note that for methods like PRF-HyDE, it
has been shown that there is marginal improvement in accuracy
when the number of candidate examples exceeds 10 [ 20]. Our results
seem to show the opposite: PRF-HyDE demonstrates improvements
up until about≈40candidate documents. This is likely due to
improvements in the ability for LLMs to handle longer contexts.
7.2 Results:Umbrela-HyDE and PRF-HyDE
with a Stronger Initial Retriever
We next explore howUmbrela-HyDE and PRF-HyDE benefit from
improving the initial retriever. As before, the results are in Table 5.
We summarize the key insights below:
•ComparingUmbrela-HyDE (row 5) with PRF-Umbrela(row 6)
suggests that if combining feedback from the LLM and corpus,
there is a modest improvement (2%) by doing so in a two-
step approach (i.e., HyDE then PRF-Umbrela), rather than
combining each source independently.
•Interestingly, under this two-step approach there is no fur-
ther improvement when “padding” PRF-Umbrelawith HyDE
vectors, as PRF-Umbrela(row 6) andUmbrela-HyDE (row 8)
are equally effective (46.2 versus 46.1). We hypothesize that
this may be due to the redundancy of terms present in the
HyDE-retrieved documents. High scoring documents retrieved
by HyDE with BM25 will likely contain similar terms to those
in the HyDE-generated documents due to the exact-matching
scoring scheme.
•The results in Section 7.1 suggested that there is no advantage to
incorporating in-context examples for HyDE. However, we find
that when using stronger in-context examples, PRF-HyDE is
more effective than HyDE. Using documents retrieved by HyDE
with BM25 as input to PRF-HyDE (row 7) yields a 1.6-point
(3.6%) improvement upon HyDE (row 2) and using documents
retrieved by Contriever MSM (row 15) yields even more gains,
with a 2.8-point (6.3%) improvement.
•It is of note that PRF-HyDE seems to yield further gains using
documents retrieved by Contriever-MSM versus documents
retrieved by HyDE despite HyDE having higher retrieval ac-
curacy. We hypothesize that the reason lies in the fact that
the documents retrieved by HyDE with BM25 are likely less
diverse(i.e., more redundant), being biased towards real docu-
ments that “look like” those sampled by HyDE, which tend to
generally cover similar content across generations. This result
shows that better retrieval does notnecessarilymean better
PRF-HyDE effectiveness and other factors may be important.
To answer RQ3, we found the answer to depend on the downstream
retriever. For dense retrieval setups, we found thatUmbrela-HyDE

8 Latency Comparisons
RQ4How does the latency vary among LLM PRF methods?
A practical consideration when utilizing LLMs for PRF is the effi-
ciency. In this section, we compare the latency of each of the LLM
PRF methods relative to HyDE. We primarily focus on three BEIR
datasets which span a range of document lengths. For documents on
the longer side, we use TREC-News, which has about 634.8 words
per document; TREC-Covid, which has 160.8 words per document,
represents the average length ( ≈200 words per document across
the 13 BEIR datasets evaluated); and lastly, for shorter documents,
we use Signal-1M, which has about 13.9 words per document [ 21].
For this experiment, we only compute the latency of the LLM com-
ponent; thus the slowdown of each method is measured based on
the ratio of its latency to that of HyDE. ForUmbrela-HyDE, we
consider its “worst-case” latency, assuming that both PRF-Umbrela
and HyDE are run one after the other, and not in parallel. Experi-
ments are run on NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation GPUs using
vLLM [11].
The results can be found in Figure 4. Overall, the results show
that each of the approaches which leverage corpus knowledge are
generally less efficient than HyDE. This slowdown increases as
more candidate documents are considered, meaning that while it
was demonstrated in Figure 3 that more candidate documents canincrease accuracy, this comes at a much slower latency with respect
to HyDE.
Comparing PRF-Umbrela, PRF-HyDE, andUmbrela-HyDE, the
answer appears to depend on the document lengths in the corpora.
For corpora which contains long documents (TREC News), the
three approaches are comparable at a lower number of candidate
documents (10), but PRF-Umbrela(and with it,Umbrela-HyDE)
begins to be much slower as the number of candidate documents
it assesses increases. Corpora with shorter documents (Signal1M)
shows a similar trend: PRF-Umbrelaand PRF-HyDE begin at a
similar latency, but PRF-Umbrelais slower at higher numbers of
candidate documents. On datasets with average length documents
(Covid), PRF-UmbrelaandUmbrela-HyDE are consistently faster
than PRF-HyDE across all amounts of candidate documents.
9 Conclusion
In this study, we conduct a large-scale and controlled evaluation
of pseudo-relevance feedback with LLMs, disentangling the inde-
pendent roles that thefeedback sourceand thefeedback model–
two central ingredients of modern PRF methods – play. Our re-
sults demonstrate that each of these dimensions influences the
downstream effectiveness of LLM PRF methods. In particular, we
found that the effectiveness of methods which leverage feedback
generated directly by an LLM (i.e., HyDE or PRF-HyDE), can be
dependent on the choice of feedback model, especially in dense
retriever settings. If utilizing feedback derived solely from – or in
combination with – corpus documents, the most important factor
is the effectiveness of the initial retriever used to obtain candidate
documents. Such methods are most effective when candidate doc-
uments are retrieved by a strong initial retriever, especially when
accounting for practical factors like latency. Overall, our results
provide afaircomparison between various LLM PRF approaches
under a unified experimental framework, providing insights for
practitioners and guiding best practices for future LLM-based PRF
approaches.

structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
This partial document discusses a systematic study of pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) with large language models (LLMs). The main points covered include:

* PRF methods built on LLMs can be organized along two key design dimensions: the feedback source and the feedback model.
* The independent role of each dimension is unclear due to entanglement in empirical evaluations.
* The study aims to fairly and systematically evaluate these design dimensions through controlled experimentation.
* The research questions addressed include the impact of the feedback model on PRF effectiveness, the choice of feedback source, the benefit of combining feedback sources, and the latency variation among LLM PRF methods.
* The study reimplements five LLM PRF methods, evaluates them on 13 low-resource BEIR datasets, and finds that the feedback model is an important design choice, feedback derived from LLM-generated text is generally more effective, and combining feedback sources can be beneficial.
* The results also show that LLM-only feedback is the most efficient, and utilizing BM25 with LLM-only feedback is a tough baseline to beat.
* The study provides a better understanding of which elements in the PRF design space are most critical and aims to guide best practices for future PRF methods built with LLMs.

structure.title: Pseudo-Relevance Feedback with LLMs
structure.summary: The partial document discusses pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) methods that utilize large language models (LLMs) for selecting or generating top-ranked documents to enrich query representation. The main points covered include the difference between traditional PRF and LLM PRF, various methods for updating query representation, and the evaluation of different PRF pipelines. The document also overviews different approaches for generating or selecting feedback documents with LLMs, including corpus-only, LLM-only, and combined corpus and LLM methods. Additionally, it discusses feedback models for updating the query representation, including the Rocchio algorithm and dense vector update methods. The overall focus is on understanding the effectiveness of different PRF methods and the improvement when combining feedback sources versus using a singular feedback source.

nodes.title: Sparse retrieval
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) methods for information retrieval, specifically focusing on the use of large language models (LLMs) to generate or select feedback documents. The main points covered include: 

1. Overview of different PRF methods, including traditional PRF, LLM-based PRF, and hybrid approaches that combine corpus and LLM-based feedback.
2. Description of various feedback models used to update the query representation, such as Rocchio and RM3 for sparse retrieval, and average vector and Rocchio vector feedback for dense retrieval.
3. Experimental setup, including the use of different retrieval models (BM25, Contriever, and Contriever MS-MARCO), LLMs (Qwen3-14B), and evaluation metrics (nDCG@20).
4. Evaluation of the effectiveness of different PRF methods and feedback models on 13 low-resource retrieval datasets from the BEIR benchmark.
5. Research questions, including the impact of the choice of feedback model on PRF effectiveness and the comparison of different PRF methods and feedback models.

The document also presents results from experiments, highlighting the benefits of using certain feedback models, such as the Rocchio algorithm, with LLM-based PRF methods, and discussing the implications of these findings for information retrieval research.

nodes.title: Dense retrieval
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses various feedback models for pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) in information retrieval. It covers the Rocchio and RM3 feedback models for sparse retrieval, and the average vector feedback and Rocchio vector feedback for dense retrieval. The document also describes the implementation of PRF methods, including PRF-Umbrela, HyDE, and PRF-HyDE, and their evaluation on 13 low-resource retrieval datasets. The main points include the comparison of different feedback models, the effectiveness of PRF methods with various retrievers, and the evaluation metrics used to assess the performance of these methods. Additionally, the document touches on the experimental setup, including the use of Pyserini, Qwen3-14B, and vLLM, and the reporting of nDCG@20 as the evaluation metric.

structure.title: Experimental Setup
structure.summary: The partial document discusses various techniques for pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) in information retrieval, including PRF-Umbrela, HyDE, and PRF-HyDE. It explores the impact of different feedback models, such as Rocchio and RM3, on the effectiveness of these PRF methods. The document also presents experimental results on 13 low-resource retrieval datasets, evaluating the performance of different PRF methods with various feedback models and retrievers, including BM25, Contriever, and Contriever MSM. The main points covered include the importance of choosing the right feedback model for LLM PRF methods, the effectiveness of Rocchio feedback algorithm for HyDE and PRF-HyDE, and the stability of PRF-Umbrela's accuracy across different feedback models.

structure.title: Varying the Feedback Model
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the main results of experiments conducted on various LLM PRF methods using different retrievers, such as BM25, Contriever, and Contriever-MSM. The results are presented in tables, showing the performance of each method on different datasets. The document also explores the effectiveness of feedback terms derived from high-quality corpus documents versus those generated by LLMs. The results suggest that feedback terms derived from LLM-generated hypothetical documents are more effective, and that the choice of feedback model can significantly impact the performance of LLM PRF methods. Additionally, the document investigates the impact of bounding the number of HyDE generations to the number of relevant documents found by PRF-Umbrela, finding that this reduces the difference in effectiveness between HyDE and PRF-Umbrela.

structure.title: Corpus-Derived vs. LLM-Generated Feedback
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the main results of various LLM PRF methods using Contriever MSM across different datasets, with a focus on comparing corpus-derived and LLM-generated feedback. The results show that feedback derived from LLM-generated hypothetical documents is more effective than feedback derived solely from corpus documents. The document also explores the impact of bounding the number of HyDE generations to the number of relevant documents found by PRF-Umbrela, suggesting that HyDE's improved effectiveness may be due to having more sources to extract feedback terms from.

nodes.title: Results: PRF-Umbrela with a Stronger Initial Retriever
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the main results of various LLM PRF methods using Contriever MSM as the retriever, with a focus on comparing the effectiveness of feedback derived from high-quality corpus documents versus LLM-generated documents. The results show that feedback derived from LLM-generated documents is more effective, with HyDE making a 1.5-point improvement over PRF-Umbrela. The document also explores the impact of the initial retriever on the effectiveness of PRF-Umbrela and HyDE, finding that stronger candidate documents returned by HyDE consistently improve the effectiveness of PRF-Umbrela. Additionally, the results suggest that feedback terms derived from corpus documents retrieved by HyDE are more useful for PRF than feedback terms generated by HyDE. The document also investigates the efficiency of judging more candidate documents to enable PRF-Umbrela to access more feedback sources, finding that judging more documents can improve the effectiveness of PRF-Umbrela, but may have efficiency implications.

nodes.title: Results: PRF-Umbrela with a Stronger Initial Retriever
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the effectiveness of LLM-PRF methods, specifically PRF-Umbrela and PRF-HyDE, in information retrieval tasks. It explores the impact of the number of top-k candidate documents on the effectiveness of these methods and compares their performance with HyDE. The results show that PRF-Umbrela's effectiveness improves as the number of candidate documents increases, eventually surpassing HyDE. The document also investigates the effect of using a stronger initial retriever on the performance of PRF-Umbrela and finds that it consistently improves the effectiveness of the method. Additionally, it compares the performance of PRF-Umbrela with different retrievers, including BM25 and Contriever MSM, and analyzes the results to understand the impact of feedback terms and query encoders on the effectiveness of the methods.

nodes.title: Results: Single Retriever Available
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a systematic study of pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) with large language models (LLMs). The study aims to understand the impact of feedback source and feedback model on PRF effectiveness. The authors evaluate five LLM PRF methods across 13 low-resource BEIR tasks and three different retrievers (BM25, Contriever, and Contriever MS-MARCO). The results show that the choice of feedback model is critical, and feedback derived solely from LLM-generated text provides the most cost-effective solution. The study also finds that combining feedback sources can improve PRF effectiveness, but the optimal strategy for combining feedback depends on the downstream retriever. Additionally, the results suggest that dense retrievers may not fully take advantage of PRF, and that feedback from high-quality corpus documents can be more effective than LLM-generated feedback if a strong initial retriever is used. Overall, the study provides insights into the design space of PRF with LLMs and guides best practices for future PRF methods.
